coredata.dc:title: A turn-off-on near-infrared photoluminescence sensor for sequential detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid based on glutathione-capped gold nanoclusters
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Wenjuan
Yu, Jiangyan
Gong, Xiaojuan
Liang, Wenting
Fan, Li
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: Many reports have suggested that near-infrared (NIR) fluorescent probes are one of the most promising molecules for improving the sensitivity of fluorescence sensing and imaging. Herein, gold nanoclusters with excellent near-infrared photoluminescence (PL) were synthesized by a simply hydrothermal treatment of hydrogen tetrachloroaurate(III) trihydrate and glutathione (GSH). The NIR PL of GSH-capped gold nanoclusters (GSH-AuNCs) can be significantly quenched by Fe3+, which follows a dynamic quenching mechanism. However, the NIR PL of the GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+ system can be recovered after the addition of ascorbic acid (AA). The decrease and increase of NIR PL intensities of GSH-AuNCs were linearly correlated with the concentration of Fe3+ and AA, respectively. Therefore, a turn-off-on NIR PL sensing strategy can be constructed for sequential detection of Fe3+ and AA with the linear range of 0.7–180 μM and 0.5–120 μM, respectively. The proposed NIR PL sensor exhibits excellent sensing performance and has been applied to the determination of Fe3+ and AA in real samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoclusters
Near-infrared photoluminescence
Fe3+
Ascorbic acid
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fe3+-modulated NIR PL “turn-on” detection of AA
Possible mechanism about sensing of Fe3+ and AA
Determination of Fe3+ and AA in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of GSH-AuNCs. Inset is the particle size distribution histogram. (B) FT-IR spectra of GSH and GSH-AuNCs.

The UV–vis absorption (a), PL excitation (b), and PL emission (c and d) spectra of the GSH-AuNCs in aqueous solution. The inset displays the pictures of GSH-AuNCs under daylight (left) and UV-light irradiation (right).

(A) NIR PL responses of the GSH-AuNCs in the presence of different metal ions (200 μM). I 0 and I are the NIR PL intensities in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively. (B) The PL emission spectra of the GSH-AuNCs with different concentrations of Fe3+ (from top to bottom: 0, 12, 24, 36, 48, 60, 72, 84, 96, 108, 120, 132, 144, 156, 168, 180, 198, 216, 234, 258, 282, 306, 330, 360, 390, 420 and 450 μM, respectively). (C) The PL intensities at 798 nm against incubation time. (D) The linear relationship between the PL intensity changes of the GSH-AuNCs and the concentration of Fe3+.

(A) The PL responses of the GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+ system towards different concentrations of AA (from bottom to top: 0, 8, 16, 24, 32, 40, 48, 56, 64, 72, 80, 88, 104, 120, 136, 152, 168 and 184 μM, respectively). (B) The relationship between the value of (I
I 0)/I 0 and the AA concentrations. The inset is the linear calibration plots of (I
I 0)/I 0 against low concentration of AA (0–120 μM). (C) The PL intensities of the GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+ system in the presence of AA (100 μM) against incubation time. (D) Selectivity of the GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+ system for the detection of AA.
Schematic illustration of turn-off-on NIR PL sensing strategy for the detection of Fe3+ and AA.
Comparison of different PL sensors for sequential determination of Fe3+ and AA.
Determination of Fe3+ in tap water real samples.
Determination of AA in VC effervescent tablets and kiwi juice real samples.

head.ce:title: A turn-off-on near-infrared photoluminescence sensor for sequential detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid based on glutathione-capped gold nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Many reports have suggested that near-infrared (NIR) fluorescent probes are one of the most promising molecules for improving the sensitivity of fluorescence sensing and imaging. Herein, gold nanoclusters with excellent near-infrared photoluminescence (PL) were synthesized by a simply hydrothermal treatment of hydrogen tetrachloroaurate(III) trihydrate and glutathione (GSH). The NIR PL of GSH-capped gold nanoclusters (GSH-AuNCs) can be significantly quenched by Fe3+, which follows a dynamic quenching mechanism. However, the NIR PL of the GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+ system can be recovered after the addition of ascorbic acid (AA). The decrease and increase of NIR PL intensities of GSH-AuNCs were linearly correlated with the concentration of Fe3+ and AA, respectively. Therefore, a turn-off-on NIR PL sensing strategy can be constructed for sequential detection of Fe3+ and AA with the linear range of 0.7–180 μM and 0.5–120 μM, respectively. The proposed NIR PL sensor exhibits excellent sensing performance and has been applied to the determination of Fe3+ and AA in real samples with satisfactory results.

The near-infrared fluorescence probes are believed to be the most promising materials for improving the sensitivity of photoluminescence sensing and imaging. In this paper, a novel turn-off-on near-infrared photoluminescence sensing strategy for sequential detection of Fe3+ and AA based on GSH-AuNCs was developed.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Metal nanoclusters, a new class of ultra-small size metal nanoparticles, have attracted increasing attention in optical sensing and imaging due to their unique photoluminescence (PL) properties [1]. These molecule-like luminescence behaviors of metal nanoclusters are attributed to the small size approaching to the Fermi wavelength of electrons and the quantization of orbital energy levels [2]. Among these metal nanoclusters, gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) are one of the most exciting nanoclusters due to their superior photostability and biocompatibility, large stokes shift, easy surface functionalization, and reduced toxicity [3,4]. AuNCs typically exhibits a core-shell structure complex consisting of core of a few to a hundred Au atoms and shell of ligand [5]. The luminescent properties of AuNCs have been proved to be related to inner core, ligand shell, size and surrounding environment. Recently, various luminescent AuNCs have been synthesized using protein [6,7], enzyme [8], amino acids [9], or other molecules [10,11] as ligands. However, most of the reported AuNCs exhibit short-wavelength emission, which greatly limits their optical application due to serious background interference. In contrast, the long-wavelength emission can significantly avoid auto-fluorescence interference because naturally molecules rarely emit fluorescence in this region [12]. Moreover, as the intensity of scattering light is inversely proportional to λ4, the interference of scattering on the fluorescence can greatly be reduced in the NIR region [13–15]. Therefore, NIR fluorescence probes are believed to be the most promising materials for improving the sensitivity of fluorescence sensing and imaging [16].

Fe3+, an essential trace element, plays an important role in several biological processes in oxygen transportation, enzyme catalysis and electron-transfer processes. It is of great significance to detect Fe3+ in clinic because the lack and over-accumulation of Fe3+ are closely related to Parkinson's syndrome, cancer or tissue damage due to the formation of free radicals [17]. Similarly, ascorbic acid (AA), an essential nutritional factor, plays an indispensable role in biological processes [18]. The abnormality level of AA can interrupt intracellular homeostasis, which is generally associated with certain diseases, such as atherosclerosis, cardiovascular disease, iron deficiency anemia, urinary stones, diarrhea, and stomach convulsion [19]. So far, a large number of methods for the detection of Fe3+ [20–22] and AA [23–25] have been reported. However, some unavoidable drawbacks such as sophisticated equipment, tedious pre-preparation procedures, time-consuming, low sensitivity, and high cost can be found in these methods.

Among these, fluorescence-based sensing strategies have drawn widespread research interest owing to their high sensitivity, simplicity, device portability, and short response time [26]. However, the performance of fluorescent sensors based on organic fluorophores or semiconductor quantum dots is affected by poor photo-stability and serious toxicity. In contrast, ultra-small PL nanoclusters can overcome the above drawbacks and become a new type of fluorophores for applications in chemical and biological sensing. Recently, a series of sensing strategy have been developed for sequential determination of Fe3+ and AA using carbon dots [27–29], gold nanoparticles [30], and conjugated polymer nanoparticles [31] as fluorescent probes. Nonetheless, these fluorescence sensors based on short-wavelength emission may suffer from serious background interference in the detection of some real samples. Therefore, there are still necessary to explore a more sensitive fluorescence sensing method for the determination of Fe3+ and AA. As far as we know, there is no report on the sequential determination of Fe3+ and AA based on the NIR fluorescence sensing strategy.

Herein, we describe a novel turn-off-on NIR PL sensing strategy for sequential determination of Fe3+ and AA based on GSH-AuNCs. GSH-AuNCs were synthesized with GSH as stabilizer and reductant, and their PL properties were studied. In order to promote its application in PL sensing, the effects of different metal ions on the NIR PL of the GSH-AuNCs were investigated. It was found that GSH-AuNCs can identify Fe3+ with high selectivity by fluorescence quenching mechanism, so a “turn-off” NIR PL sensing platform for Fe3+ detection was constructed. Subsequently, the NIR PL intensity of the GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+ system was recovered by adding AA, so a “turn-on” NIR PL sensing strategy based on Fe3+-mediated GSH-AuNCs is developed for the detection of AA. The NIR PL response principle is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

Glutathione (reduced type, GSH) was purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate(III) trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O, 99.9+%) was obtained from Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI, USA). HgCl2, Ni(NO3)·6H2O, ZnSO4·7H2O, NaCl, MgSO4, CaCl2, CuSO4·5H2O, KBr, AgNO3, MnSO4·H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, PbCl2, Fe(NO3)3 were bought from Tianjin Chemical Plant (Tianjin, China). Ultrapure water from Milli-Q purification system was used in all of the aqueous solutions.

TEM images were obtained by a JEOL JEM-2100F (Tokyo, Japan). The FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Bruker Tensor II FTIR spectrometer (Bremen, Germany) at 500–4000 cm−1. The UV–visible absorption spectra were performed on a Lambda 365 UV–visible absorption spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) at 200–800 nm. The NIR PL spectra were measured by a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrometer (Palo Alto, CA, USA) with the excitation and emission slit widths of 20 nm. The time-resolved fluorescence spectra were recorded by a FLS 920 spectrometer (Edinburgh, British) and the fluorescence lifetimes were obtained through the instrument-specific software.

The GSH-AuNCs was prepared according to previous report with some modifications [32]. In brief, 23.6 mg HAuCl4 and 17.5 mg GSH were dissolved in 14.3 mL and 5.7 mL ultrapure water, respectively. Then, the above solution were mixed and moved to Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave at room temperature. The autoclave was placed in an oven and heated at 120 °C for 1.5 h. The resulting mixture was dialyzed in 500 Da for 2 h to purify the GSH-AuNCs. Finally, the solution was lyophilized to GSH-AuNCs powder.

The as-prepared GSH-AuNCs was accurately weighed and dissolved in ultrapure water to prepare 5 mg/mL of stock solution for further use. For the determination of Fe3+, different amounts of Fe3+ stock solution were mixed with 1 mL of GSH-AuNCs stock solution and diluted to 2.5 mL with PBS (0.01 M, pH 7.4). The NIR PL spectra of the mixture solution were recorded after 30s of incubation time. The excitation wavelength was set at 520 nm with excitation/emission slits of 20 nm.

Determination of AA with the GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+ sensor system was conducted in PBS (0.01 M, pH 7.4). The GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+ sensor system was obtained based on the concentration of GSH-AuNCs and Fe3+ of 2 mg/mL and 180 μM respectively. Different concentration of AA solution was added into the above sensor system with 18 min of incubation time. Then NIR PL spectra of the mixture were recorded with the excitation wavelength of 520 nm and excitation/emission slits of 20 nm.

The as-prepared GSH-AuNCs were placed onto the holey carbon-covered copper grids and the morphology, particle size and dispersion was observed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1A, the GSH-AuNCs are spherical shape and uniformly dispersed. The particle size distribution of the GSH-AuNCs (the inset of Fig. 1A) is in the range of 1.0–4.0 nm with an average diameter of 2.36 ± 0.40 nm, which is calculated by 120 counts of random particles. FT-IR spectroscopy was performed to inquire into the surface functional groups of the as-prepared GSH-AuNCs. As can be seen from Fig. 1B that the characteristic absorption peak at 2523 cm−1 belongs to –SH stretching vibration is found to disappear in the FT-IR spectrum of GSH-AuNCs compared with GSH. It means that the SH bond of GSH is cleaved, and then stable AuS bond is formed, which leads GSH to immobilize the surface of GSH-AuNCs. These results reveal that GSH has been used as a reductant and stabilizer to successfully prepare GSH-AuNCs.

To explore the optical properties of the GSH-AuNCs, UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were depicted in Fig. 2 . The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the GSH-AuNCs (curve a, Fig. 2) shows that the absorption intensity decreases with the increase of wavelength and a shoulder peak at 310 nm in the UV region, then the absorption curve tends to be smooth in the visible region. The typical surface plasmon resonance absorption peak of larger-size gold nanoparticles at 520 nm does not appear, suggesting that the as-prepared nanomaterials are gold nanoclusters with smaller particle size [33]. The excitation spectra of GSH-AuNCs (curve b, Fig. 2) displays that the GSH-AuNCs possess two excitation peaks at 415 and 520 nm. Accordingly, the emission spectra of GSH-AuNCs with the excitation wavelengths of 415 and 520 nm were obtained respectively, as shown in curve c and d in Fig. 2. It is gratifying that the PL emission peak of GSH-AuNCs is located at 798 nm in the NIR region. Moreover, it is noting that the PL emission peak of GSH-AuNCs does not shift under the different excitation wavelengths. Considering the interference of frequency doubling peak, the excitation wavelength of 520 nm was selected in the subsequent experiments. The photo of GSH-AuNCs under 365 nm ultraviolet irradiation exhibits strong red luminescence (the inset of Fig. 2). It has been demonstrated that the PL emission of GSH-AuNCs at 798 nm is independent of the size of nanoparticles, but affected by GSH surface coverage and Au bonding environment [34]. The PL quantum yield of as-prepared GSH-AuNCs was calculated to be 4.18% based on the comparative method using cresyl violet as the standard, as shown in Fig. S1. The NIR PL properties of GSH-AuNCs can promote it as an excellent fluorescent probe.

The fluorescence sensors for the detection of metal ions based on various fluorescent nanomaterials have been constructed. However, there are still gained much attention on detecting metal ions by NIR fluorescence sensing [16]. Herein, in order to explore the application of GSH-AuNCs in NIR PL sensing, the effects of different metal ions on its NIR PL were carried out. Fig. 3A displays the relative PL intensity of GSH-AuNCs (I/I 0) in the presence of 14 types of metal ions, respectively. It is obvious that the addition of Fe3+ results in the remarkable PL quenching of GSH-AuNCs. In contrast, except that Cu2+ can slightly quench the PL of the GSH-AuNCs, other metal ions hardly affect the PL of the GSH-AuNCs. The results indicate that the GSH-AuNCs can be used to identify Fe3+ with high selectivity based on a “turn-off” NIR PL sensing strategy.

In order to evaluate the detection sensitivity, the NIR PL spectra of the GSH-AuNCs in the presence of different concentration of Fe3+ were recorded. It can be seen from Fig. 3B that with the increase of the concentration of Fe3+, the NIR PL intensities of the GSH-AuNCs gradually decrease, and the emission peak of the GSH-AuNCs does not shift. Also, the NIR PL response to Fe3+ was very fast, that is, the maximum quenching effect was achieved within 30 s (Fig. 3C). The effect of pH on the NIR PL detecting Fe3+ was also investigated. Firstly, it was found that the NIR PL intensity of GSH-AuNCs can maintain a strong and stable level in the range of pH 4.0–10.0 (Fig. S2A). Therefore, the NIR PL intensity of the GSH-AuNCs in the above pH range was estimated after adding 100 μM Fe3+. As shown in Fig. S2B, the pH value of the solution has a negligible effect on the behavior of the sensing system. Thus, neutral pH 7.4 was selected as the pH value of the Fe3+ detection system.

The relationship between the NIR PL intensity ratio I 0/I and the concentration of Fe3+ was evaluated under the optimal experiment conditions (Fig. 3D), where I and I 0 represent the NIR PL intensity of the GSH-AuNCs in the presence and absence of Fe3+, respectively. A good linear relationship can be obtained in the range of 0.7–180.0 μM with a correlation coefficient R 2 = 0.9980. The limit of detection (LOD) of Fe3+ was calculated to be 0.07 μM (S/N = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+-modulated NIR PL “turn-on” detection of AA
ce:section.ce:para: According to Fig. 3A, compared with the strong quenching effect of Fe3+, Fe2+ has little effect on the NIR PL of the GSH-AuNCs. As is known to all, AA has strong reducibility which can reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+. Naturally, AA was considered to be added into the GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+ system to observe their NIR PL spectra further. As expected, Fig. 4A shows that the NIR PL of the GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+ system can be recovered after adding AA. When AA was added into the GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+ system, the effect of incubation time on the NIR PL intensity of the system was studied and the results were shown in Fig. 4C. The NIR PL intensity of the system at 798 nm is gradually recovered with the increase of incubation time and remains at a relatively stable value after 18 min. Thus, 18 min was chosen as the optimal incubation time and carried out in subsequent experiments.

After that, it is observed that the NIR PL intensity of the GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+ system is higher with the increasing of the amount of AA. Finally, when AA of 184 μM was added into the sensing system, the recovery rate of NIR PL intensity can be up to 98%. Fig. 4B displays that the relationship between the NIR PL intensity changing value (I
I 0)/I 0 and the concentration of AA, where I and I 0 represent the NIR PL intensity of the GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+ system in the presence and absence of AA, respectively. A linear correlation between the plot of (I
I 0)/I 0 and the concentration of AA in the range of 0.5–120 μM (R 2 = 0.9968) was observed (Fig. 4B). The limit of detection (LOD) of AA was calculated to be 0.03 μM (S/N = 3). Therefore, the NIR PL “turn-on” strategy for the detection of AA is developed based on Fe3+-mediated GSH-AuNCs system.

It is well known that selectivity is also an essential indicator for the evaluation of detection methods. Therefore, some representative interferents including UA, Glc, Glu, Gly, Val, Ser, Trp, NAC, Cys, Phe, Ala and His were used to measure the anti-interference of the sensor for AA determination, and the results are shown in Fig. 4D. It is observed that these selected interfering substances have no obvious enhancement effect on the NIR PL intensity of the GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+ sensing platform, implying good selectivity and anti-interference of the proposed sensor. To evaluate the repeatability of the sensor in detecting AA, 10 parallel measurements were performed. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated to be 3.52%, indicating good repeatability of the proposed sensing method.

Furthermore, the performance of the sensor for the sequential detection of Fe3+ and AA was compared with the previous reported PL sensing methods and summarized in Table 1 . These previously reported PL sensing strategies for the sequential detection of Fe3+ and AA are based on the fluorescence emission in the visible region. In this work, the sensor based on NIR emission possess low LOD, wide detection rang, and fast response time, which is comparable to the previous methods or even more superior than most of the reported fluorescence sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism about sensing of Fe3+ and AA
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1, the NIR PL intensity of the as-prepared GSH-AuNCs can be intensively quenched by Fe3+. To investigate the quenching mechanism, the time-resolved PL spectra of the GSH-AuNCs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ were performed. The fluorescence lifetime was fitted by the intensity decay equation and the relevant data displays in Table S1. The fluorescence lifetimes of the GSH-AuNCs significantly decrease with the increasing concentration of Fe3+ from 0 μM to 200 μM, indicating an ultrafast GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+ electron-transfer process. Fig. S3 shows the relative change of fluorescence lifetime (τ 0/τ) against the concentration of Fe3+, and the plot was fitted by the Stern-Volmer equation, τ 0/τ = 1 + Ksv[Q]. The good linear relationship between τ 0/τ and the concentration of Fe3+ also indicates that the quenching behavior follows a dynamic quenching mechanism [39]. It is well known that Fe3+ can coordinate bond with oxygen atom of carbonyl groups and nitrogen atom of amino groups [40]. That is, oxygen and nitrogen atoms in the GSH on the surface of the AuNCs can provide electrons to Fe3+. Meanwhile, other GSH in GSH-AuNCs may also occupy other sites of six-coordinated Fe3+ through the same coordination bond, which induces the cross-linking of GSH-AuNCs and further result in the NIR PL quenching. However, the following dynamic quenching mechanism implies that no complex is formed between Fe3+ and the excited GSH-AuNCs, and only random collision occurs. Therefore, it is speculated that the effective interaction between Fe3+ and carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of the GSH-AuNCs facilitates the non-radiative electron transfer process [41]. In this way, the electrons in the excited state of GSH-AuNCs may transfer to the half-filled 3d orbits of Fe3+, resulting in the NIR PL quenching.

The Fe3+-modulated GSH-AuNCs sensing platform exhibits a sensitive response to AA based on the fluorescence recovery mechanism. It is speculated that a stronger binding affinity between AA and Fe3+ than that between the GSH-AuNCs and Fe3+, so Fe3+ can be pulled away from the surface of the GSH-AuNCs. Meanwhile, Fe3+ can be reduced to Fe2+ by the oxidation-reduction between Fe3+ and AA [19,27]. As a result, non-radiative electron transfer from GSH-AuNCs to Fe3+ can be minimized, and then the NIR PL intensity of the GSH-AuNCs was recovered by AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fe3+ and AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical application of the proposed sensor, the analysis of Fe3+ and AA in the real samples was carried out, respectively. The tap water samples were collected from our laboratory at Shanxi University and filtered by a 0.45-μm membrane. The treated samples were added to the sensing system of GSH-AuNCs, and the content of Fe3+ was determined according to the proposed method. Then the Fe3+ spiked samples of three different concentrations were added, the recovery rates were calculated. As shown in Table 2 , a good recovery rate in the range of 97.07–102.84%, suggesting the validity of our sensing system for Fe3+ detection.

The proposed sensing platform of the GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+ was applied for the detection of AA in the VC effervescent tablets and fruits juices purchased from the local pharmacy and supermarket (Taiyuan, Shanxi). The specification of VC effervescent tablets shows that each 1 g VC tablet contains 100 mg of AA. A known concentration of AA solution was prepared using VC effervescent tablet and added into the GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+ sensing system. The concentration of AA was estimated and the results were summarized in Table 3 . After three parallel determinations, the concentrations of AA were found to be 49.54, 49.99 and 50.13 μM, respectively. Subsequently, the content of AA in VC effervescent tablets was further calculated, and the results revealed that it is almost consistent with the instruction. For the detection of AA in fruits juices, 0.25 mL of a kiwi juice after centrifugation was added into the GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+ probe solution to detect the concentration of AA and the results are shown in Table 3. The amount of AA in kiwi juice was estimated to be 6.14 mg/100 mL. Then the recovery tests were performed using spiked method to evaluate the accuracy of this sensing strategy. The recovery values were ranging from 96.62 to 104.91% with the RSD of less than 7.0%, demonstrating that the sensing platform can provide reliable data for the detection of AA in real samples.

In summary, the as-prepared GSH-AuNCs with excellent NIR PL properties were demonstrated the high selectivity for Fe3+ recognition by a dynamic fluorescence quenching of non-radiative electron transfer mechanism, so a“turn-off”sensing platform for Fe3+ determination was constructed. Upon addition of AA to the GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+ system, the NIR PL intensity was recovered, which is attributed to the reducing action of AA making Fe3+ exfoliate from the surface of the GSH-AuNCs and revert to Fe2+, so a “turn-on” sensing platform for AA determination was developed. More significantly, this sensing can be used for the determination of Fe3+ and AA in real samples with satisfactory results. The proposed NIR sensing strategy would have wide application prospect and great versatility in the development of other novel biological and environmental sensors.

I have made substantial contributions to the conception or design of the work; or the acquisition, analysis, or interpretation of data for the work. I have drafted the work or revised it critically for important intellectual content. I have approved the final version to be published. I agree to be accountable for all aspects of the work in ensuring that questions related to the accuracy or integrity of any part of the work are appropriately investigated and resolved.

All persons who have made substantial contributions to the work reported in the manuscript, including those who provided editing and writing assistance but who are not authors, are named in the Acknowledgments section of the manuscript and have given their written permission to be named. If the manuscript does not include acknowledgments, it is because the authors have not received substantial contributions from non-authors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21705101, 21976113 and 81901814), Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No. 201801D121040) and Shanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Project (NO. 201903D121109).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.119085. Quantum yield calculation of GSH-AuNCs, pH influences on GSH-AuNCs and GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+, the plot of the fluorescence lifetimes against the concentration of Fe3+, and table containing fluorescence lifetimes of GSH-AuNCs with increasing concentration of Fe3+, respectively.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gold nanoclusters for biomedical applications: toward in vivo studies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Porret, X. Le Guevel, J.L. Coll, Gold nanoclusters for biomedical applications: toward in vivo studies, J. Mater. Chem. B 8 (2020) 2216. DOI: 10.1039/c9tb02767j

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tailoring the photoluminescence of atomically precise nanoclusters

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Kang, M. Zhu, Tailoring the photoluminescence of atomically precise nanoclusters, Chem. Soc. Rev. 48 (2019) 2422. DOI: 10.1039/c8cs00800k

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Y.G. Srinivasulu, Q. Yao, N. Goswami, J. Xie, Interfacial engineering of gold nanoclusters for biomedical applications, Mater. Horiz. 2020, Advance article. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1039/d0mh00827c.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Du, R. Jin, Atomic-precision engineering of metal nanoclusters, Dalton Trans. 49 (2020) 10701. DOI: 10.1039/d0dt01853h

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: R. Dalapati, S. Biswas, Aqueous phase sensing of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid by a metal-organic framework and its implication in the construction of multiple logic gates, Chem. Asian J. 14 (2019) 2822–2830. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1039/c9nr01826c.

P.H. Chan, Y.C. Chen, Human serum albumin stabilized gold nanoclusters as selective luminescent probes for Staphylococcus aureus and methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus, Anal. Chem. 84 (2012) 8952–8956. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1021/ac302417k.

X. Le Guevel, N. Daum, M. Schneider, Synthesis and characterization of human transferrin-stabilized gold nanoclusters, Nanotechnology 22 (2011) 275103. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1088/0957-4484/22/27/275103.

N. Alkudaisi, B.A. Russell, D.J.S. Birch, Y. Chen, Lysozyme encapsulated gold nanoclusters for probing the early stage of lysozyme aggregation under acidic conditions, J. Photochem. Photobio. B 197 (2019) 111540. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotobiol.2019.111540.
